slides.title: Nothing Great Is Built Alone
slides.subtitle: A year of building computing innovations starts with the skill they're all built on
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Welcome to AP Computer Science Principles — this is the first content lesson of the whole course. This year students will study computing innovations from every angle: how they're built, how they store and move data, how their algorithms work, and how they change the world — and in the spring, every student builds one of their own for the Create Performance Task. The College Board opens the course here, with collaboration, for a reason: every innovation students will study or build is the product of people working together. Today defines what a computing innovation even is, then shows why teams — real teams, not divided labor — build better ones. The culture this class builds today is the culture the Create task will run on in April.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain what makes something a computing innovation, and how computing innovations are improved through collaboration (LO CRD-1.A)
Explain how computing innovations are developed by groups of people, using online collaboration tools and models like pair programming (LO CRD-1.B)
Demonstrate effective interpersonal skills — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation — during collaboration (LO CRD-1.C)

slides.script: Three targets, one per learning objective. First, the vocabulary that anchors the entire course — what qualifies as a computing innovation — plus the claim that collaboration improves them. Second, the how: the tools and models real development teams use, including pair programming. Third, something unusual: LO CRD-1.C is demonstrated, not just explained — students practice interpersonal skills live today, in the bell ringer and the discussion windows, because the College Board assesses that objective through doing, not through multiple-choice questions.

slides.prompt: Your school is replacing the paper lunch line with a lunch-ordering app. Round 1: list every need a user of this app might have. Go for quantity — every kind of user, every situation you can imagine.
slides.subprompt: Round 2: combine lists with a partner. Count the needs your partner caught that you missed — and the ones only you caught.
slides.directions: Keep both counts. Be ready to share the best need on your merged list that neither of you had alone.
slides.script: Two minutes for round one, solo — quantity over polish: allergies, free-lunch privacy, a la carte, ordering for a sick sibling, no smartphone, dietary and religious restrictions, staff who eat at a different time. Then two minutes for round two: partners merge lists and count what each person missed. Nearly every pair discovers the same thing — the merged list is dramatically better than either solo list, and the best items often came from the partner's different lunch experience. That discovery IS today's lesson before it has a name: a second perspective caught needs the first one structurally could not see. Keep the merged lists visible; they come back in every section today, and the misses are exactly what the CED calls bias.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have 4 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: What Counts as a Computing Innovation
sections.description: The program-integral test and the three shapes an innovation can take (EK CRD-1.A.1–A.2).

sections.title: Why Collaboration Improves Innovations
sections.description: Diverse perspectives, bias, and why developers consult users (EK CRD-1.A.3–A.6).

sections.title: How Real Teams Collaborate
sections.description: Online tools, pair programming, and the four interpersonal skills (EK CRD-1.B.1–B.2, C.1).

sections.title: Deep Dive: Open-Source Collaboration
sections.description: How thousands of strangers who never meet build the world's most important software.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 1.1 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Today's Guided Notes map: four sections. Three are green and CB-required — the computing-innovation test, why collaboration improves innovations, and how real teams actually work together. The fourth is the blue deep dive on open-source software: not required by the College Board, but it shows every idea from this lesson operating at internet scale. Packets stay open all period; a digital copy lives on the Topic 1.1 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com for anyone absent. This is the routine for every lesson this year, so today is also a dress rehearsal for how the class runs.

slides.label: What Counts as a Computing Innovation
slides.kicker: The Program-Integral Test · Learning Objective CRD-1.A
slides.script: Section one. Before anyone can explain how computing innovations are improved, the class needs the term itself — it is the single most reused phrase in this course, appearing in Big Ideas 1, 2, 4, and 5 and in all three innovation investigations this year. The test is one sentence long, and the classification has exactly three shapes. Students who lock this in today will recognize it on exam questions in May.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO CRD-1.A & CRD-1.B)

terms.definition: An innovation that includes a program as an integral part of its function; it can be physical (e.g., a self-driving car), nonphysical computing software (e.g., picture-editing software), or a nonphysical computing concept (e.g., e-commerce) (EK CRD-1.A.1, CRD-1.A.2).
A common collaboration model in which two programmers build one program together at one computer — one writes while the other actively reviews each line, and the roles rotate (EK CRD-1.B.2).

A slant or blind spot built into an innovation when the perspectives shaping it are too similar; collaboration that includes diverse perspectives helps avoid bias in the development of computing innovations (EK CRD-1.A.4).

slides.script: Three terms carry the day. Computing innovation is the course's anchor term, and its test is one clause: a program must be an integral part of its function — remove the program and the thing stops being what it is. Pair programming is the College Board's named example of a collaboration model, and the definition already contains the exam's favorite trap: the second programmer actively reviews, never passively watches. Bias here is not a courtroom word — it is a design defect: the lunch-app needs each student missed solo were bias waiting to be shipped. Students write every definition in the packet; that is the standing rule for every vocabulary section this year.

slides.heading: One Test, Three Shapes
slides.subheading: Every computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function (EK CRD-1.A.1) — but it can wear three very different bodies (EK CRD-1.A.2).

items.title: Physical
items.summary: Hardware you can touch, with a program integral to how it works. A self-driving car is the CED's example: strip out the software and it is just a car — the innovation lives in the program.

items.title: Nonphysical software
items.summary: The innovation IS the program. Picture-editing software is the CED's example: there is no gadget at all, just code that does something new.

items.title: Nonphysical concept
items.summary: An idea or way of doing things that exists only because programs enable it. E-commerce is the CED's example: 'buying things online' is not an object or a single app — it is a concept powered by many programs.

slides.script: Three cards, three shapes, one shared test. The physical card surprises students most — yes, a car can be a computing innovation, as long as a program is integral to its function. The software card is the shape students default to, so it needs the least selling. The concept card is the one the exam uses to catch people: e-commerce is nothing you can touch or install, yet the CED explicitly names it a computing innovation, because the entire idea only exists through programs. The move to model out loud: for any candidate, ask 'is a program integral to its function?' first, and only then ask which shape it is.

slides.heading: Innovation or Not? Run the Test
slides.context: For each candidate, apply EK CRD-1.A.1 — is a program an integral part of its function? — then classify it using EK CRD-1.A.2's three shapes.
rows: Yes — software that steers, brakes, and navigates is integral to its function
Yes — the innovation IS a program
Yes — the whole idea exists only because programs enable it
No — no program is integral to its function
Yes — an onboard program collects and analyzes the readings
slides.script: Five candidates, and students complete the blanked cells in the packet before answers are revealed. The self-driving car is worked as the model: program integral, physical shape. The picture-editing app and e-commerce fill the other two CED examples. The bookshelf is the negative control — it fails the test at step one, and the type column should say so rather than force-fitting a shape. The smartwatch is the transfer check: physical object, but the heart-rate function is the program, so it classifies exactly like the car. The reasoning column matters more than the verdict column — on the exam, the justification is what separates the right answer from a lucky guess.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — The Innovation Test
slides.subheading: These map to LO CRD-1.A. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: An old vending machine drops a snack using a purely mechanical coin lever. A newer model runs software that tracks stock and accepts phone payments. Apply the test from today: which one is a computing innovation, and why?
E-commerce is not an object and not an app you can install, yet the College Board calls it a computing innovation. Explain what makes that classification work.
Invent your own example of each shape — one physical, one software, one concept — that did not appear on today's slides.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 1.1 page.
slides.script: Five minutes, full sentences on the Guided Notes. One: the mechanical machine fails the test — no program anywhere — while the newer model passes because software is integral to how it now works; same snack, different classification. Two: the concept shape exists precisely for cases like e-commerce — the innovation is a way of doing things that only programs make possible. Three is the creativity check, and the concept column is where students struggle; streaming, ride-sharing, and online banking are the kinds of answers to steer toward. A few students will be called on to defend their invented examples before the next section.

slides.label: Why Collaboration Improves Innovations
slides.kicker: Diverse Perspectives, Users, and Bias · Learning Objective CRD-1.A
slides.script: Section two — the claim of the whole topic. The bell ringer already proved it experimentally: two perspectives produced a better requirements list than one, every single time. Now the CED names the mechanism: diverse perspectives improve the product and defend it against bias, and the people who use the program are a perspective the team must actively go get. This is not a soft-skills sermon — it is a claim about product quality.

slides.heading: Why Diverse Teams Build Better Innovations
bullets: Effective collaboration produces a computing innovation that reflects the diversity of talents and perspectives of those who designed it (EK CRD-1.A.3).
Collaboration that includes diverse perspectives helps avoid bias in the development of computing innovations (EK CRD-1.A.4) — a team that shares one blind spot ships that blind spot.
Consultation and communication with users are important aspects of development (EK CRD-1.A.5) — the team is never the whole audience.

Information gathered from potential users helps developers understand the purpose of the program from diverse perspectives, and develop a program that fully incorporates those perspectives (EK CRD-1.A.6).
slides.script: Four EKs, one argument. Start from the bell ringer: each solo list reflected one lunch experience, and the merged list reflected two — that is EK A.3, the product reflecting the talents and perspectives of everyone who designed it. A.4 gives the stakes: the needs a homogeneous team never thinks of become bias in the shipped product — students without smartphones simply cannot order lunch. A.5 and A.6 extend the merge beyond the team: users, including POTENTIAL users who do not use the program yet, hold perspectives no team meeting can generate, and the information gathered from them is used to understand the program's purpose and fold those perspectives into the design. The emphasis worth repeating: diversity here is an engineering strategy, not just a value statement.

capture.q: Diverse perspectives on a team protect the PRODUCT by ______.
capture.a: keeping one group's blind spots from being built into the innovation

capture.q: Consultation and communication with ______ are important aspects of developing a computing innovation.
Talking to potential users before building matters because ______.
capture.a: their perspectives reveal what the program must actually do for them

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Whose Perspective Is Missing?
slides.subheading: These map to LO CRD-1.A. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: In the bell ringer, your merged list contained needs you missed alone. Connect that experience to today's claim: what does effective collaboration produce that solo work usually cannot?

A team of expert programmers — all the same age, from the same neighborhood — builds a city transit app that works badly for wheelchair users and night-shift workers. Using the word bias, explain how the team's makeup shaped the product, and state the fix.
Why does the College Board insist on gathering information from POTENTIAL users — people who don't use the program yet — rather than only its current fans?
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 1.1 page.
slides.script: Five minutes, full sentences. One: the merged list is the miniature proof of EK A.3 — the product of collaboration reflects perspectives no single author had. Two: the transit team's identical vantage point became the app's assumptions; that is bias entering through team makeup, and the fix is twofold — diversify the perspectives in the room and consult the users who were designed around. Three is subtle and worth pausing on: current fans already fit the product, so their feedback confirms it; potential users reveal the purposes and barriers the product has not yet served, which is exactly what EK A.6 says the gathered information is for. Strong answers to prompt three deserve a public read-aloud.

slides.label: How Real Teams Collaborate
slides.kicker: How Real Teams Work · Learning Objectives CRD-1.B & CRD-1.C
slides.script: Section three turns from why to how. Groups of people develop computing innovations using two kinds of machinery: technical machinery — online tools for sharing work and giving feedback, and models like pair programming — and human machinery, the four interpersonal skills the CED names. The section's central contrast is the difference between a group that divides work and a team that actually collaborates, because on the exam and in this classroom those are not the same thing.

slides.heading: Group Work vs. True Collaboration
slides.subheading: Both put four names on the project. Only one produces an innovation that reflects all four minds (EK CRD-1.A.3).

left.title: Just dividing the work
left.definition: Split the project into parts, work alone, staple the pieces together at the deadline
examples: No one ever sees, questions, or improves anyone else's part
The product reflects four separate minds — never a team
Disagreements are dodged by never talking at all

right.title: True collaboration
right.definition: Perspectives mix DURING development — every part is shaped by more than one mind
examples: Online tools let programmers share and provide feedback on ideas and documents (EK CRD-1.B.1)
Pair programming: one types while the other reviews every line — and the roles swap (EK CRD-1.B.2)
Friction is worked through with interpersonal skills: communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation (EK CRD-1.C.1)

slides.footer: AP TIP: On the exam, 'collaboration' means perspectives mixing during development. An answer choice that just divides tasks is describing group work, not collaboration.
slides.script: This is the contrast most school group projects get wrong. The left column feels efficient and fair, but run it through EK A.3: a project assembled from four sealed compartments reflects four individuals, not a team's combined perspective — and every blind spot survives untouched. The right column is what the CED actually describes: online tools exist specifically so programmers can share work AND give feedback on it — sharing without feedback is just a file transfer; pair programming builds the perspective-mixing into the keyboard itself; and the four interpersonal skills are what a team uses instead of avoidance when perspectives collide. The uncomfortable truth to say out loud: true collaboration includes productive disagreement — a team with zero friction is usually a team that divided the work.

capture.q: You can tell true collaboration happened because the final product ______.
capture.a: shows fingerprints of every designer's talents, not just one person's

capture.q: Online tools support collaboration by allowing programmers to ______ on ideas and documents.
Splitting the work into silos fails as collaboration because ______.
capture.a: the skills that mix perspectives (consensus, negotiation) never get used

slides.heading: “Collaboration Means Splitting Up the Work”
slides.misconception: A good team divides the project into equal parts, each member builds their part alone, and the pieces get combined at the end — that's what collaborating means.
slides.whyItHappens: Dividing tasks feels fair and efficient, and it conveniently avoids the uncomfortable parts of teamwork — disagreement, feedback, and compromise. Most school group projects quietly run this way, so students arrive believing it is the definition.
slides.correction: Effective collaboration produces an innovation reflecting the diversity of EVERYONE who designed it (EK CRD-1.A.3), through interpersonal skills: communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation (EK CRD-1.C.1). Pure division of labor removes exactly the interactions that improve the product.
slides.example: In the bell ringer, merging lists with a partner surfaced needs each person missed alone. A 'split the work and never talk' team never merges — so every blind spot survives into the final product, unchallenged.
slides.script: This misconception deserves its own slide because nearly every student walks in holding it. The tell is what division of labor eliminates: if no one ever reads, questions, or pushes back on anyone else's part, then no perspectives ever mix, and EK A.3's promise — a product reflecting the whole team — is structurally impossible. Notice the CED's list of interpersonal skills: consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation. Those three only exist where disagreement exists, which tells you the College Board expects collaboration to include friction, handled well. Students explain in the packet why the myth fails; the strongest explanations will use their own bell-ringer merge as evidence.

slides.heading: Deep Dive: Collaboration at Internet Scale
slides.subheading: The CED never names specific tools — but open source shows every EK from today running among thousands of strangers.

left.title: The problem
left.definition: Thousands of volunteer programmers, spread across every time zone, who will never meet
examples: Linux, Wikipedia's software, and the Python language are all built this way
No shared classroom, no shared schedule — only shared tools
Every idea from today still applies; it just has to survive at scale

right.title: How open source solves it
right.definition: Online tools turn sharing-plus-feedback into an entire engineering culture
examples: Version control (e.g., GitHub) tracks every change and lets anyone propose one
A 'pull request' is shared work plus structured feedback — EK CRD-1.B.1 at internet scale
Code review runs on written communication, consensus building, and negotiation (EK CRD-1.C.1)

slides.footer: Deep dive only — the AP exam never asks about brand-name tools; it tests WHAT online tools make possible: sharing and feedback.
slides.script: A deep dive to make today's EKs feel less like classroom rules — this is beyond what the exam requires. The world's most important software is built by people who have never been in the same room: a contributor proposes a change as a pull request, reviewers comment on it line by line in writing, and nothing merges until the discussion converges — sharing plus feedback, exactly what EK B.1 says online tools are for. The interpersonal skills go fully written: a code-review thread is communication, consensus building, and negotiation in text form, archived forever. For students who want to see it, real pull-request threads on major projects are public — reading one is watching CRD-1.C.1 happen between strangers.

capture.q: A pull request is shared work plus ______.
capture.a: structured feedback from other programmers before the change is accepted

capture.q: Open-source teams practice interpersonal skills mostly through ______.
capture.a: written communication (code review, comments, discussion threads)

capture.q: The AP exam tests what online tools make possible, never ______.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — The Team Check
slides.subheading: These map to LO CRD-1.B and CRD-1.C. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: A four-person team 'collaborates' by splitting an app into four files and combining them the night before the deadline. Explain what their final product will fail to reflect, using today's language.

Two pair-programming setups: in one, the navigator scrolls a phone while the driver types; in the other, the navigator questions every line. Which pair is running the real model, and what is the passive pair losing?
Your team deadlocks 2–2 on a design choice. Name two interpersonal skills from today you could apply, and describe one concrete move for each.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 1.1 page.
slides.script: Six minutes, full sentences. One: the stapled-together app reflects four individuals but not the diversity of talents and perspectives of a team — EK A.3 is the vocabulary the answer needs. Two: the questioning navigator is the real model from EK B.2; the passive pair is losing the live review — bugs seen but unspoken, designs never challenged — which is the entire reason the model exists. Three: any two of the four skills work, but the moves must be concrete — consensus building might mean finding the goal both sides share; negotiation might mean each side conceding the feature it values less; and gathering user information is the tiebreaker that turns an argument into evidence. These answers preview the discussion debrief at the end of class.

slides.heading: Common AP Traps
slides.subheading: Three ways Topic 1.1 loses points on the exam — one minute now, real points in May.

strategies.name: “It must be a gadget”
strategies.text: Wrong test. An innovation just needs a program integral to its function — picture-editing software and e-commerce count; a bookshelf never will (EK CRD-1.A.1-A.2).

strategies.name: “Diversity is only fairness”
strategies.text: The CED's claim is about the PRODUCT: diverse perspectives help avoid bias in the innovation itself (EK CRD-1.A.4). Answer in terms of the artifact, not the team.

strategies.name: Passive pair programming
strategies.text: Both programmers are engaged — the navigator actively reviews every line while the driver types. 'One works, one watches' is the distractor (EK CRD-1.B.2).

slides.script: One minute, three traps. Trap one: students see 'car' or 'refrigerator' in an option and disqualify it — but physical objects with integral programs are computing innovations, and so are pure concepts like e-commerce; the program-integral test is the only test. Trap two: exam items about diverse teams reward the bias-avoidance mechanism — the product misses fewer users — not generic statements about fairness; the right option almost always mentions perspectives, blind spots, or users. Trap three: distractors love the sleeping navigator — any option where the second programmer is passive, or where the 'pair' works separately, contradicts EK B.2. Students write each trap in their own words in the packet.

slides.heading: Collaboration, in One Slide
points: A computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function — and it can be physical, nonphysical software, or a nonphysical concept (EK CRD-1.A.1–A.2).
Effective collaboration produces an innovation that reflects the team's diversity of talents and perspectives, and diverse perspectives help avoid bias (EK CRD-1.A.3–A.4).
Consultation and communication with users — including potential users — reveal a program's purpose from diverse perspectives and shape its design (EK CRD-1.A.5–A.6).
Online tools support collaboration through sharing AND feedback; pair programming is a common model that facilitates it (EK CRD-1.B.1–B.2).
Effective teams practice interpersonal skills: communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation (EK CRD-1.C.1).
slides.next: Topic 1.2, Program Function and Purpose: what a program actually is, what inputs and outputs are, and how to describe what code does — the first step toward writing your own.
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide, and the whole course in miniature. The innovation test — program integral to function, three possible shapes — will be reused all year, including in the three innovation investigations. Collaboration improves innovations through a concrete mechanism: diverse perspectives catch what any single perspective misses, which is how bias gets avoided, and users are a perspective teams must actively consult. Teams run on online tools built for sharing plus feedback, on models like pair programming, and on four named interpersonal skills. Next class opens with the Topic 1.1 quiz, then Topic 1.2 zooms into the program itself. Before packing up, students run the exit check below — any unchecked box is tonight's review target on the Topic 1.1 page. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.
ican: classify a computing innovation as physical, software, or concept (LO CRD-1.A)
explain how diverse perspectives improve a product and help avoid bias (LO CRD-1.A)
describe how teams collaborate: online tools and pair programming (LO CRD-1.B)
name and use the four interpersonal skills of effective teams (LO CRD-1.C)

quiz.title: Topic 1.1 — Collaboration — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 7 questions, 12 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A classmate argues that a self-driving car cannot be a computing innovation 'since an innovation has to be software, and a car is a physical machine.' Per EK CRD-1.A.1 and CRD-1.A.2, what is the best response?
options: The classmate is right: computing innovations are always nonphysical, so a physical car can never qualify
The classmate is wrong: a computing innovation can be physical, as long as a program is an integral part of its function — which is true of a self-driving car
The classmate is wrong: every modern machine counts as a computing innovation, whether or not it runs a program
The classmate is right about the car, and e-commerce also fails to qualify, since neither one is a piece of installable software
questions.rationale: EK CRD-1.A.2 explicitly lists the physical shape and names the self-driving car as its example; the qualifying test is EK CRD-1.A.1 — a program integral to the function — and the car's driving software passes it. A states the exact misconception the EK refutes: 'computing innovation' is not a synonym for 'software.' C overcorrects by dropping the test entirely — a machine with no integral program (a purely mechanical device) does not qualify. D compounds the error: e-commerce is the CED's own example of a nonphysical computing CONCEPT, so it qualifies without being installable software.

questions.stem: Which of the following lists a physical computing innovation, a nonphysical computing software innovation, and a nonphysical computing concept — in that order (EK CRD-1.A.2)?
options: E-commerce; a self-driving car; picture-editing software
Picture-editing software; e-commerce; a self-driving car
A self-driving car; e-commerce; picture-editing software
A self-driving car; picture-editing software; e-commerce
questions.rationale: EK CRD-1.A.2 assigns each example to a shape: the self-driving car is physical (hardware with an integral program), picture-editing software is nonphysical computing software (the innovation IS the program), and e-commerce is a nonphysical computing concept (an idea enabled by programs). A leads with e-commerce as physical — a concept you cannot touch. B calls e-commerce software, but e-commerce is not a single installable program; it is a way of doing business that many programs enable. C swaps the last two: e-commerce is not software and picture-editing software is not a concept — it is a concrete program.

questions.stem: A four-person team — all recent graduates of the same program, all fluent smartphone users — builds a medication-reminder app. Testing reveals it is nearly unusable for elderly patients, its largest intended audience. Per EK CRD-1.A.4, which change to the development process most directly addresses the root cause?
options: Include collaborators and consulted perspectives from outside the developers' own demographic, since diverse perspectives help avoid bias
Hire additional developers with the same strong qualifications so the interface can be rebuilt more quickly
Add more features to the app so that at least some of them will appeal to elderly patients
Accept the result, since developers cannot reasonably be expected to anticipate the needs of every possible user
questions.rationale: The root cause is bias: four near-identical perspectives baked their own assumptions into the interface, and EK CRD-1.A.4 says collaboration that includes diverse perspectives helps avoid exactly this. B adds more of the same perspective — speed increases, but the blind spot survives, so the rebuilt interface repeats the failure. C treats the symptom with volume: more features designed from the same vantage point are just more misses. D abdicates: the CED provides the remedy D claims is impossible — consultation with users (EK CRD-1.A.5) and information gathered from potential users (EK CRD-1.A.6) exist precisely so teams are not limited to what they can personally anticipate.

questions.stem: Before writing any code for a new school-lunch-ordering app, the development team surveys and interviews students, cafeteria staff, and parents. Per EK CRD-1.A.6, what does the information gathered from these potential users allow the team to do?
options: Prove ahead of time that the finished program will contain no errors when it is eventually written
Skip user testing later in development, as early opinions make further verification unnecessary
Understand the purpose of the program from diverse perspectives and develop a program that fully incorporates those perspectives
Guarantee the app will be profitable by identifying in advance which users would pay the most for it
questions.rationale: C is EK CRD-1.A.6 nearly verbatim: information gathered from potential users is used to understand the purpose of a program from diverse perspectives and to develop a program that fully incorporates these perspectives. A promises something no consultation can deliver — error-free code is a testing-and-development concern, not a survey outcome. B inverts EK CRD-1.A.5: consultation and communication with users are important ASPECTS of development, ongoing rather than a one-time replacement for testing. D swaps the CED's purpose-and-perspectives rationale for a profit motive the EK never mentions.

questions.stem: According to EK CRD-1.B.1, how do online tools support collaboration among programmers?
options: By automatically merging every team member's code so programmers never need to review each other's work
By allowing programmers to share and provide feedback on ideas and documents
By replacing the need for interpersonal skills such as communication and negotiation
By ensuring each programmer works on a separate part of the project without seeing the others' files
questions.rationale: B is EK CRD-1.B.1 verbatim: online tools support collaboration by allowing programmers to share and provide feedback on ideas and documents — both halves matter, sharing AND feedback. A removes the feedback half by automating it away; review is the point, not an inconvenience to eliminate. C confuses the tool with the team: EK CRD-1.C.1's interpersonal skills are still practiced through online tools (written communication, negotiation in review threads), never replaced by them. D describes isolation — the divide-and-never-look model — which is the opposite of what collaboration tools exist to enable.

questions.stem: Two students adopt pair programming for a project. Which description shows the model working as the CED intends (EK CRD-1.B.2)?
options: One student writes the entire program alone while the other waits to write the documentation afterward
The stronger programmer always types while the other silently watches, careful never to interrupt the flow
The two students split the program in half and work at separate computers, combining their files at the end
One student types while the other actively reviews each line and suggests improvements, and the two swap roles regularly
questions.rationale: EK CRD-1.B.2 names pair programming as a common model that exists to FACILITATE COLLABORATION — D is the only option where collaboration actually happens: two active minds on the same code, live review, and rotating roles so both perspectives shape the program. A is solo development with a documentation assistant — the second perspective never touches the code. B is the classic trap: a permanently silent watcher provides no review, so the pair gains nothing over a solo programmer except an audience. C is division of labor at separate computers — the very arrangement pair programming was designed to replace.

questions.stem: Which of the following are interpersonal skills that effective collaborative teams practice, per EK CRD-1.C.1? I. Consensus building II. Conflict resolution III. Dividing the project so that team members never need to communicate
options: I and II only
I only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: EK CRD-1.C.1 lists the interpersonal skills of effective collaborative teams: communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation — so I and II are both on the list. III is the opposite of an interpersonal skill: engineering the project so communication never happens removes the interactions where perspectives mix, undermining EK CRD-1.A.3's requirement that the product reflect the whole team. B wrongly drops conflict resolution — the CED expects teams to work THROUGH disagreement, not avoid it. C and D both accept III, which mistakes communication-avoidance for teamwork.

guide.title: Topic 1.1 — Collaboration — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Big Idea 1: Creative Development · Topic 1.1 · 1 class period + assessment block
overview: Topic 1.1 is the opening content lesson of the course, and it does double duty: it introduces the course's anchor term — computing innovation, defined by the program-integral test and classified into three shapes (physical, nonphysical software, nonphysical concept) — and it establishes the collaborative culture the whole year runs on, from daily pair tasks to the Create Performance Task in the spring. Students learn the CED's actual claim about teamwork: diverse perspectives improve the product and help avoid bias, users (including potential users) are a perspective teams must actively consult, online tools exist for sharing plus feedback, pair programming is the named collaboration model, and effective teams practice four interpersonal skills — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation.

The through-line is the bell ringer: students list user needs for a school-lunch-ordering app alone, then merge lists with a partner and count what each missed. The merged list IS the lesson before it is named — collaboration producing a product no single perspective could, and the solo misses previewing bias. One assessment note: LO CRD-1.C carries skill 6.A (Collaborate in the development of solutions), which the College Board does not assess on the multiple-choice exam. The quiz therefore tests the EK content behind it (what the skills are, how pair programming works), while the LO itself is demonstrated live — in the bell ringer, the discussion windows, and every pair task this year.
los: LO CRD-1.A — Explain how computing innovations are improved through collaboration.
LO CRD-1.B — Explain how computing innovations are developed by groups of people.
LO CRD-1.C — Demonstrate effective interpersonal skills during collaboration.
eks: EK CRD-1.A.1 — A computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function.
EK CRD-1.A.2 — A computing innovation can be physical (e.g., self-driving car), nonphysical computing software (e.g., picture editing software), or a nonphysical computing concept (e.g., e-commerce).
EK CRD-1.A.3 — Effective collaboration produces a computing innovation that reflects the diversity of talents and perspectives of those who designed it.
EK CRD-1.A.4 — Collaboration that includes diverse perspectives helps avoid bias in the development of computing innovations.
EK CRD-1.A.5 — Consultation and communication with users are important aspects of the development of computing innovations.

EK CRD-1.A.6 — Information gathered from potential users can be used to understand the purpose of a program from diverse perspectives and to develop a program that fully incorporates these perspectives.
EK CRD-1.B.1 — Online tools support collaboration by allowing programmers to share and provide feedback on ideas and documents.
EK CRD-1.B.2 — Common models such as pair programming exist to facilitate collaboration.
EK CRD-1.C.1 — Effective collaborative teams practice interpersonal skills, including but not limited to: communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation.
materials: Printed Topic 1.1 quiz (student copies) for the assessment block.
Scratch paper or index cards for the bell-ringer list merge (each student needs their own solo list before pairing).
prereqs: None — opening lesson of the course. The bell ringer assumes no prior computer science; it builds the case for collaboration from a task any student can attempt cold. If class accounts and the website walkthrough happened during course launch, students can find the Topic 1.1 page immediately; otherwise point them to it at the exit check.

pacing.focus: The computing-innovation test, why collaboration improves innovations, and how real teams collaborate (LO CRD-1.A, CRD-1.B, CRD-1.C)

segments.segment: QOTD (BI1/T1.1/Q1) + bell ringer (the lunch-app list merge)
What counts as a computing innovation: vocab, three shapes, classification table (EK CRD-1.A.1–A.2)
Stop and Think: the innovation test
Why collaboration improves innovations: perspectives, bias, users (EK CRD-1.A.3–A.6)
Stop and Think: whose perspective is missing?
How real teams collaborate: group work vs. collaboration + misconception (EK CRD-1.B.1–B.2, C.1)
Stop and Think: the team check

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Topic Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run the Topic 1.1 quiz as the opener of the next class (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: “Collaboration means splitting up the work”
misconceptions.myth: A good team divides the project into equal parts, everyone builds their part alone, and the pieces are combined at the end.
misconceptions.reality: EK CRD-1.A.3 says effective collaboration produces an innovation reflecting the diversity of talents and perspectives of everyone who designed it, and EK CRD-1.C.1's skills — consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation — only exist where team members actually interact and disagree. Pure division of labor removes the perspective-mixing that improves the product. The bell-ringer merge is the standing counterexample: the value appeared at the moment the lists combined.

misconceptions.heading: “A computing innovation has to be a gadget (or has to be an app)”
misconceptions.myth: Students disqualify candidates that are physical objects ('a car is just a machine') or that are not installable software ('e-commerce is not a real innovation — it is just shopping').
misconceptions.reality: The only test is EK CRD-1.A.1: a program integral to the function. EK CRD-1.A.2 then names all three shapes explicitly — physical (self-driving car), nonphysical software (picture-editing software), and nonphysical concept (e-commerce). The worked table's bookshelf row shows the honest negative: no integral program, no computing innovation.

misconceptions.heading: “More teammates automatically means a better product”
misconceptions.myth: If collaboration improves innovations, then adding more people — any people — must improve them more.
misconceptions.reality: The CED's mechanism is diversity of perspectives, not headcount: EK CRD-1.A.4 credits DIVERSE perspectives with avoiding bias, and EK CRD-1.A.5–A.6 add consultation with users as a perspective source the team cannot supply itself. Four copies of the same perspective add speed, not insight — the medication-app quiz item (Q3) is built directly on this myth.

support: Give a sentence frame for the classification test: 'A ______ is / is not a computing innovation because a program is / is not an integral part of its function; its type is physical / software / concept.' Students run every candidate through the frame before free-form answers.

Post the four interpersonal skills (communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation) as an anchor chart; during any pair task this year, students point to the skill they are currently using when asked.
extension: Have deep-dive students find a real open-source project they already use (a language, a game mod, a browser extension), read one actual pull-request thread, and tag each comment with the EK CRD-1.C.1 skill it demonstrates.

Challenge students to find a genuinely borderline candidate (smart water bottle, QR-code menu, digital textbook, e-sports) and argue BOTH sides of the program-integral test before ruling — the arguing is where the EK gets internalized.

discussion: Think of an app you use daily that clearly wasn't designed for someone you know — a grandparent, a younger sibling, a friend without reliable data. What perspective was missing from the team, and how would consulting that user have changed the product?
Pair programming halves the number of keyboards in use, yet schools and companies swear by it. What exactly does the navigator produce — and when is that worth more than a second typist?

Which interpersonal skill from the CED's list — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, or negotiation — is hardest for teams your age, and what norm could this class adopt to make it easier?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
